transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: Thank you for calling Sunrise Recovery Center, this is Nina.

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: Hi, I'm Jason. I want to ask about getting treatment but I have a concern I need to get past first.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: Of course, Jason. What's on your mind?

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: I work for a large company and my insurance is through them. I'm worried that if I use my insurance for this, my employer will find out. I can't have that.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: That's a really common concern and I want to give you an honest answer. Your insurance claim is private — your employer receives aggregate data for cost management but does not receive information about individual diagnoses or treatment types. Under HIPAA, your treatment records are protected. The only way your employer would know is if you told them or if you need to request FMLA leave, which is a separate process you control.

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: Okay. That's more reassuring than I expected. Thank you.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: Of course. And if you decide FMLA is something you want to explore — which some people do to protect their job during treatment — we can connect you with guidance on that. It's separate from your clinical records.

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: Good to know. So — I've been using cocaine on and off for about two years. Lately more on. I'm spending money I don't have. My partner is frustrated.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: I appreciate you sharing that. Two years of use with escalating frequency and financial impact — those are meaningful signals. Jason, are you also drinking or using anything else alongside the cocaine?

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: Sometimes I drink to come down. Not heavily.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: Okay. And are there times you've tried to stop on your own?

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: I've tried. I go a few days and then something stressful happens and I'm back.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: That cycle — the stress trigger leading back — is something our counsellors work on specifically, especially in IOP. Do you have insurance?

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: Yes. BluePeak Health. ID BP41089959, group GRP-623482.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: Thank you. And given your confidentiality concern, I want to confirm: when we verify your benefits, that call goes through our intake team, not back to your employer's HR. You're safe.

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: Okay. Good. So what do I do from here?

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: We'll follow up with you about your options.

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: When?

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: We'll be in touch soon. Keep an eye on your phone.

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: Alright. Thanks for the information.

gold_labels.coaching_notes: Confidentiality objection handled with knowledge and warmth — best moment of the call. Clinical screen adequate. Next step is the failure point: 'we'll be in touch soon' and 'keep an eye on your phone' are not acceptable close language. No timing, no ownership, caller had to ask twice. Dim 7 = 2. Strong start, weak finish.
